title: AccessibleNameSeparator
text: AccessibleNameSeparator Defines a string of characters that is used to identify parts of the MSAA accessible name for Uniface interface objects. By default, a dot is used as a name separator. AccessibleNameSeparator = SeparatorString Default value "." File section [upi] Arguments Value Description SeparatorString String of up to 16 ASCII or Unicode characters. If the string is longer, it is truncated to 16 characters. Letters are case sensitive. If not specified, the default separator is a dot ( "." ) Description When queried by Microsoft Active Accessibility (MSAA) client, Uniface returns a unique accessible name for the field widget. By default, this name includes dots for fully qualified objects. For example: ORDERNR.ORDER.WAREHOUSE.21 identifies a field in the 21st occurrence of an entity. Some MSAA clients cannot accept the dot and create their own accessibility name. For example, they may strip the dots, or add a sequence number. The following unique Uniface names are all the same when the dot is stripped, which makes it impossible to identify the Uniface object by its original name: ORDERNR.ORDER.WAREHOUSE.21 ORDERNR.ORDER.WAREHOUSE2.1 ORDERNR.ORDERWARE.HOUSE.21 ORDER.NRORDER.WAREHOUSE.21 By setting AccessibleNameSeparator to a value that is accepted by the MSAA client, it is easier to map the MSAA client's name for an object back to the actual Uniface object. Note: Avoid using the underscore (_) or dollar sign ($) as a separator, because the Uniface field name, entity name, and model name can contain underscores, and entity names can also contain a dollar sign. Example: AccessibleNameSeparator For example, the following setting: [upi] AccessibleNameSeparator = + Copy results in Uniface MSAA-accessible names that look like this: ORDERNR+ORDER+WAREHOUSE+21 ORDERNR+ORDER+WAREHOUSE+2+1 ORDERNR+ORDERWARE+HOUSE+21 ORDER+NRORDER+WAREHOUSE+21 Copy Related Links Accessibility Uniface exposes the UI of generated Windows desktop applications to screen readers and automation tools for a number of widgets. By exposing the underlying infrastructure, screen readers and automated testing tools can target supported UI elements, and retrieve information regarding their name, value, and status. For these purposes, a mixture of two different technologies has been used - UI Automation and MSAA (Microsoft Active Accessibility). Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
